HE quest to develop autonomous robotic cooking systems
1
1  is driven by the fast-paced demands of contemporary life.
. T
In a world where time is often scarce, and labor shortages and
5
aging populations are becoming pressing societal issues, these
0
4 robots stand not only as a testament to technological efficiency
2 but also as a beacon for the goal of enhanced quality of life.
:
They offer invaluable assistance to those with limited cooking
v
i skills or physical challenges, providing a sense of indepen-
X dence and access to a variety of culinary experiences. Beyond
r
personal use, these robots have the potential to revolutionize
a
the culinary industry, enabling restaurants and food services
to enhance efficiency, maintain consistent quality, and explore
new recipes. Moreover, they align with societal goals such as
reducing food waste and improving cooking energy efficiency.
For  these reasons, Developing a robotic cooking system
has been actively explored in the robotics field since  the
1980s. The beginnings of kitchen automation were marked
by the development of robots such as Suzumo’s sushi-making
machines, which  focused on specific and  repetitive  tasks
requiring precision. These early robots were foundational,
setting the stage for more complex developments. Moving into
the 1990s and early 2000s, the field of culinary robotics began
to specialize, seen in companies such as Robot Coupe, which
automated tasks such as chopping and blending. This era was
characterized by the introduction of equipment that, while not
fully-fledged robots, represented significant strides in kitchen

1
king System Utilizing a
hen and a Dual-Arm
Manipulator
Gillespie, Yeting Liu, Dennis Hong
automation. The mid-2000s to early 2010s saw an expansion
of the horizons of culinary robotics, with the introduction of
specialized robots and conceptual prototypes. This period was
marked by experimentation, with robots such as Briggo Coffee
Haus, now known as Costa Coffee BaristaBot,  automating
coffee preparation and signaling the potential for robotics in
various culinary applications. The 2010s were a decade of
rapid advancements in culinary robotics. This period saw the
introduction of robots like Flippy by Miso Robotics, designed
for specific tasks like burger flipping, and marked a significant
leap in the capabilities and applications of cooking robots.
Consumer-focused innovations also emerged during this time,
targeting both commercial and home markets and expanding
into diverse culinary areas. In the late 2010s and early 2020s,
the integration and sophistication of cooking robots became
more pronounced. Robots such as Samsung’s Bot Chef and
Moley’s Robotic Kitchen, equipped with advanced technology
and AI, offered adaptable and sophisticated cooking assis-
tance, reflecting a shift towards enhancing user experience and
versatility. This era also witnessed an expansion in service
robots, indicating a trend towards comprehensive automation
in the food service industry. However, most of the cooking
robots mentioned above are single-taskers, specialized only for
a single action like chopping, frying, grilling, etc. Samsung’s
Bot Chef and Moley’s Robotic Kitchen are aiming to be multi-
tasking versatile robotic cooking systems. However, they are
yet in a conceptual development phase and are notable to show
start-to-finish autonomous cooking process.
Today’s cooking robots are actively trying to utilize ad-
vanced tactile sensors, force/torque sensors, and camera sen-
sors such as RGB-D cameras or LiDAR sensors, as well as
increasingly leveraging AI and machine learning in order to
enhance personalization and adaptability. The use of collabora-
tive robots (cobots) is also an effort to create flexible cooking
robot  systems  that can interact accurately and safely with
objects and humans in dynamic environments. Thus, efforts
towards the automation of cooking that began in the early
1980s have become much more sophisticated over the last 40
years, replacing many simple labor-intensive tasks. However,
there is still a long way to go before fully replicating the intri-
cacies and complexities seen throughout cooking operations.
In this article, we present our autonomous robotic cooking
system, YORI (Yummy Operations Robot Initiative), devised
to tackle these challenges through a fusion of cutting-edge
robotics  technologies and an innovative approach aimed at
simplifying cooking tasks. The overview of  the system is

A. Dual-Arm Manipulator
Following advancements  in safety and sensing technolo-
gies,  cobots emerged  in  the  late 1990s and early 2000s.
Unlike traditional  industrial  robots,  cobots are designed to
work alongside humans, marking a significant  turning point
in robotics engineering. Equipped mainly with force-torque
sensors and advanced algorithms,  cobots can perceive and
adapt  to their surroundings. This adaptability makes cobots
suitable for various tasks requiring human-robot  interaction,
such as assembly, sorting, and inspection.
However, despite these advantages, cobots face limitations
when used in dynamic environments due to their slow ac-
celeration and high stiffness, typically seen in industrial ma-
nipulators. Moreover, with recent  trends favoring the use of
drones, wheeled robots, and legged robots, cobots are still
less utilized due to being considered heavy and less mobile.
In light of these challenges, research is underway to design
lighter arms that are backdrivable, compliant, and conducive
to force control. Notable examples include the quasi-direct
drive manipulator called BLUE [2] developed at Berkeley and
the low inertia high backdrivability tendon-driven manipulator
LIMS [3] developed at KoreaTech.
B. Proprioceptive Actuator
To enable the backdrivability and compliance of the manipu-
lator, one of the most efficient solutions is using proprioceptive
actuators. Proprioceptive actuators are usually composed of
a high torque, brushless DC motor and a gearbox with less
than 10:1 reduction to ensure adequate output  torque and
high torque transparency [4]. Unlike traditional high-geared

servo motors which have a high stiffness and payload but
with the drawbacks of high reflected inertia due to friction
losses and low compliance to unknown forces, proprioceptive
actuators are excellent in backdrivability and impact mitigation
which help the hardware to be compliant to disturbances [5].
Moreover,  the output  torque of proprioceptive actuators can
be easily estimated from the current driving them, by virtue
of the torque transparency, which helps to simplify the overall
hardware system and the control strategy.
C. Robotic Cell Layout Design
Historically, the concept of layout optimization has had its
roots in manufacturing and industrial  facility design, where
the arrangement of equipment  and workflow is critical  to
operational success. In extending these principles to robotics,
robotic cell layout design needs to consider the unique con-
straints of  robotic systems. Considering the complexity of
robot movements, especially in environments with multiple
robots or  those that  include interactions with humans, ne-
cessitates sophisticated models and algorithms to solve the
optimization problems inherent in workspace design [6].
Recent  advancements  in computational power and opti-
mization algorithms have enabled more complex robotic cell
layout design problems to be addressed. Techniques such as
Mixed Integer Linear Programming, Genetic Algorithms, and
Convex Optimization are frequently employed to find optimal
or near-optimal  solutions  to  these problems. Furthermore,
simulation-based optimization and digital  twin technologies
have provided platforms for testing and validating workspace
designs virtually before physical implementation.
D. Manipulator Control
Controlling a manipulator equipped with proprioceptive ac-
tuators requires a highly accurate model. This ensures precise
force and position feedback, enabling the system to adapt and
respond effectively to dynamic environments. Such a model
is pivotal  in achieving the delicate balance between respon-
siveness and stability and is essential  for advanced robotic
applications where precision and reliability are paramount.
In the context of this requirement, the application of inverse
dynamics controllers for tracking purposes becomes integral.
These controllers leverage this accurate model to predict the
necessary torques to achieve the desired trajectories, ensuring
high precision in motion control [7]. Concurrently, for interac-
tion tasks, impedance controllers allow for the safe and natural
interaction with dynamic environments and human operators
[8].

Both control strategies are rooted in the precise modeling
of the manipulator and exemplify the critical role of advanced
control techniques in enhancing the manipulator’s adaptability
and performance in diverse applications.
Inverse dynamics control in the joint space is as follows:
T
τd = M(q)q¨+C(q, q˙) + g(q) + J Ftip  (1)
where M(q) is the mass matrix, C(q, q˙) is the term containing
Coriolis and centripetal effects, g(q) is the gravitational terms,
J is the Jacobian, and Ftip is the end-effector contact forces.
The friction term was neglected for simplicity.

3
Impedance control  in Cartesian space  is  shown  in  the
equation below:
τd = M(q)q¨+C(q, q˙) + g(q)
T
+ J  (K(xd − x) +D(x˙ d − x˙ ) + Ftip)  (2)
where M(q), C(q, q˙),  g(q), K, D are  the mass matrix,
Coriolis and centripetal terms, gravitational terms, the stiffness
matrix,  the damping matrix,  respectively. Ftip  is  the end-
effector contact forces, and x, x˙ are the position and velocity
in the Cartesian space.
E. Motion Planning
Addressing the challenge of navigating both arms of a
manipulator without collisions in compact and dynamic envi-
ronments has led to numerous attempts utilizing optimization-
based methods. However,  the nonconvex nature of these op-
timization problems often renders them extremely difficult to
solve, even locally. As a result, sampling-based methods have
been employed, despite their  limitations in managing high-
dimensional  spaces and continuous differential  constraints
effectively. To surmount  these challenges,  recent  advance-
ments have showcased the efficacy of  techniques aimed at
finding the shortest paths in Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS)
[9], demonstrating notable performance improvements. While
the necessity for continued research and application of such
techniques to enhance system perfection is acknowledged, this
work has made strides in simplifying complexities through
modular kitchen design and layout processes. Consequently,
it has been possible to fundamentally eliminate concerns re-
garding the reachability of the manipulator, dynamic obstacles,
and collisions between both arms. This approach facilitated
the rapid online computation of  smoother  trajectories  that
minimize jerk and significantly enhance system usability.
By nature, our brain tries to maximize the smoothness of
our trajectories. When considering how an actual human arm
moves from one point to another, Flash and Hogan observed
experimentally that human generates smooth trajectory that
minimizes  jerk, which is defined as  the rate of change of
acceleration. Their work modeled an optimization model that
minimized the L2 norm of  the jerk profile of a trajectory
[10]. Furthermore, Yazdani proposed that minimizing the L∞
norm of  the jerk yields a ”bang-bang” type control policy,
which makes more biological sense since the central nervous
system uses bang-bang control policies as well [11]. Similarly,
we implemented this minimum jerk optimization method to
generate smooth trajectories for both arms.
F. Task Scheduling
As automation extends to increasingly more complex and
time sensitive  tasks, proper  task scheduling and real  time
adaptability becomes crucial for producing a robust and ef-
ficient system. This can especially be seen when examining
the automation of commercial kitchens. The kitchen can be
broken down to a finite state machine composed of a series of
heterogeneous robots (kitchen appliances) each with their own
unique capabilities. Orders are sent to the system with a set list.

During the early stages of  the project, we developed a
prototype dual-arm manipulator  using servo actuators  for
the verification of performing cooking tasks using robotic
manipulators [13]. However, due to the usage of rigid servo
actuators and lack of force/torque sensors,  the robot manip-
ulator was unable to complete certain tasks where a precise
torque estimation or rapid movement was required, such as
cutting and tossing food. Furthermore, since all  the compo-
nents including actuators and structural parts are off-the-shelf
products without any design optimizations, the payload at the
end effector was limited and the overall workspace was not
ideal. With the numerous constructive experiences from the
servo motor actuated manipulator prototype, we developed
the next-generation proprioceptive dual-arm manipulator using
high-performance quasi-direct drive actuators. These actuators
allow for the manipulators compatibility with many cooking
tasks, in particular those requiring impacts and precise force
control, while being compliant to changes in the constructed
environment.
1) Hardware  Configuration:  Unlike  traditional  fully-
actuated robotic manipulators with 6 or 7 DoFs in each arm,
the YORI dual-arm manipulator platform has a total of 11
DoF,  including one torso roll  joint  in the center, and two 5
DoF arms on either side. The 5 DoF arm has the pitch and
yaw joints in the shoulder, one elbow pitch joint, and a 2 DoF
wrist  including a pitch and roll  joint. The main purpose of
having 5 DoF arms instead of fully actuated 6 DoF arms is to
cut the mass of the arm. This favors highly dynamic manip-
ulation tasks and increases the payload for more generalized
cooking tasks.  In addition,  there is a customized pneumatic
tool changer at the wrist to adapt multiple cooking implements
for performing different tasks.
Considering the dual-arm manipulator as a multitasking tool
for cooking, the length of each link is decided based on the
human upper body proportions [14]. The length of the upper
arm and forearm are 400 mm and 375 mm, respectively. The
clavicle link length is 420 mm, so that both arms can not only
work together on the same task but also individually work on
separate tasks. Carbon fiber tubes are used for all the arm links

4
Fig. 2.  Dual-arm manipulator with proprioceptive actuators and a linkage
mechanism, designed for dynamic and dexterous manipulation with low arm
inertia.
due to their advantages of having a light weight, high stiffness,
and high durability. To reach all the kitchen appliances in the
constructed environment,  the total height of the platform is
about 1300 mm. Table I lists the major mechanical parameters
of the dual-arm manipulator.
A. Dual-Arm Manipulator

Wrist mass  733 [g]  Wrist length  85 [mm]
1) Actuation selection: To effectively choose the correct
size of the actuators in each joint,  joint torque estimation is
necessary at the beginning of the hardware design process. As
a cooking dual-arm manipulator whose workspace is mainly
in the sagittal plane, the pitch actuators at the shoulder, elbow
and wrist joints will endure the most torque from the payload.
Thus a static moment balance approach is used to estimate the
worst-case scenario loading conditions on the pitch actuators
when  the manipulator  is  fully extended  in  the horizontal
direction [13]. The calculated maximum torque load for the
shoulder, elbow and wrist pitch joints are 37.93 Nm, 20.29
Nm and 3.749 Nm,  assuming a 3 kg payload attached to
the end-effector with a safety factor of 1.5. To satisfy the
torque requirements from the static analysis,  three types of
proprioceptive actuators fromWestwood Robotics are adopted,
the specifications of these actuators are listed in Table. II.

TABLE II

The actuator selections are verified in simulation by ap-
plying the physical properties of the actuators and the links
throughout the joint trajectories of multiple cooking tasks. The
Table. IV shows the average and maximum torque exerted at
each joint with a 5 kg payload, which shows that the selected
proprioceptive actuators are suitable for the desired working
scenarios.
2) Joint  and  linkage:  As  the dual-arm manipulator  is
designed to be dynamic and proprioceptively actuated,  it  is
desired to be lightweight and have a low inertia. Therefore,
we  looked  into different  actuator distribution solutions  to
keep all of the heavy actuators close to the torso instead of
being directly located at each joint like in traditional robotic
manipulators. The torso yaw, shoulder yaw and shoulder pitch
actuators are directly mounted to the corresponding joints,
because their locations are already close to the torso. However,
the elbow pitch joint actuator was relocated to a position near
the shoulder joint so that the distal mass of the manipulator
can be reduced. As for the wrist pitch and roll joints, since the
actuators for both joints are relatively small and lightweight,
we decided to keep them at the joint in favor of having a clear
design and reducing overall system complexity.
Since the elbow pitch actuator was relocated to the shoulder,
a mechanism needed to be introduced to transfer the torque
from the actuator to the elbow pitch joint. Generally, a timing
belt is a good option for power transmission due to its sim-
plicity and ability to transmit continuous rotation. However,
due to its low stiffness,  there will be unwanted compliance
between the belt teeth and the pulley. As a result,  the rotor-
belt  resonant  frequency will be low, which could limit  the
torque control bandwidth of the joint [15]. To overcome this
problem, a reliable torque transmission with high stiffness is
used, a linkage mechanism.

5
Fig. 3. Tool Changer Mechanism: (a) A guiding cone aligns the tool changer
to correct grasping position, (b) Tool changer’s fingers are actuated by using
compressed air to grip, (c) Fully locked tool changer and tool plate
As shown in Fig. 2, we utilize two pairs of 4-bar linkage
mechanisms both in a parallelogram configuration to properly
transmit the torque from the actuator to the elbow joint with a
1:1 transmission ratio. The double 4-bar linkage mechanism’s
design is configured to maximize the elbow range of motion
and eliminate the singularity of the elbow joint when the arm
is fully extended.
3) Camera: The camera attached on the torso is an Intel
RealSense D435. The set RGB frame resolution was 640 x
480 at 30 frame rate, and the RGB sensor resolution is 2-
megapixel. The Intel RealSense D435 is also an active stereo
depth camera with an ideal range of 0.3 m to 3 m, which can
be utilized for future research.
4) Tool Changing System: A manipulator with a general-
purpose end-effector  could be designed  to handle several
tasks, but would eventually limit the scope of the system. A
manipulator that can utilize multiple specialized end-effectors
is capable of being more effective  in being updatable  to
handle any number of  tasks [16]. To allow YORI  to better
handle various tools for different tasks, YORI was utilized a
pneumatic tool changer system. This allowed YORI to swap
end-effectors between various kitchen utensils including a pan,
a container, a knife, and a meat tenderizer. Each tool employs
a custom tool plate, which the tool changer’s fingers can grip
and lock onto using compressed air as shown in Fig. 3. A
guiding cone feature allows the end-effector  to correct any
offset between the correct grasping position and the current
position before the finger surface touches the tool plate. From
our experiments,  the tool changer could successfully guide
itself to the locking position on the tool plate with up to 8
mm of translational offset from the center and with up to 10
degrees of angular offset.
B. Modular Kitchen
One of the key features of YORI is its modular kitchen,
which, through the design of the cooking cell, creates an or-
ganized environment instead of a cluttered one. The controlled
environment enhances the system’s reliability, and the modular
configuration increases the system’s scalability. To optimally
design such a kitchen environment layout, it was possible to
formulate an optimization problem through simple definitions,
which are as follows: In (a) of Fig.

4, the volume and location
of an appliance are depicted by a red cubic as seen in A, while

The objective function aims to minimize the total distance
from each appliance’s key point to the center of the manipu-
lator’s workspace, optimizing for efficiency and accessibility.
The ellipsoidal workspace constraint ensures that all appli-
ances’ toolplate points, where the toolchanger should grasp,
fall within the manipulator’s operational space, modeled as
an ellipsoid. Non-overlap constraints, implemented through
the f function, prevent physical interference among the

1) Rotating Mixer: From breading chicken wings to cook-
ing stir  fry,  the mixing and tumbling of  ingredients, with
or without applied heat,  is a task common to many recipes.
Several commercial products were identified but were deemed
incompatible due  to  the need  to easily remove  the  food
touching mixing drum for proper cleaning. Similar cooking

7
automated kitchen cell. Included are the a) spice dispenser and its ingredient
mixer and the detachable pot, d) the induction pan and matching squeegee, d)
e deep fryer and water boiler with their associated cooking basket, and h) the
actions were also described in literature using manipulator
arms to manually rotate the cooking device [18], however,
while feasible,  this would limit  the ability of the system to
multitask cooking operations. Thus a dedicated mixer making
use of a modular rotating drum was conceived of and designed.
Seen in Fig. 5 section c)i,  the design has  two axes of
rotation which allows  for multiple movement patterns  for
different functions (i.e. a set angle for stir frying or a swinging
motion for better mixing salads). The modular cooking drum,
shown in Fig. 5 section c)ii, allows the utilization of multiple
drums specialized with various sizes and internal features for
different  tasks, easy cleaning through the removal of soiled
drums from the system, and more flexibility for transporting
and transferring ingredients. The rotation about  the drum’s
primary axis is indirectly driven through a gear train spinning
interlocking magnetic components along the interfacing edges
of the pot and the mixer. This ensures ease and repeatability
of insertion and removal while minimizing the potential for
slipping or  jamming. A simple magnetic encoder system is
incorporated into the large gear to provide feedback on the
spinning speed of the drum and accurately align the drum for
removal. Lastly an IR thermal camera allows for the device to
track the temperature of the drum without requiring physical
contact.
2) Food Processor: Another ubiquitous cooking task we
addressed was the slicing and dicing of ingredients. A common
approach explored in both commercial products and literature
is  the use of a spinning blade wheel which can be tuned
and optimized to achieve specifics shapes and dimensions.
Direct cutting performed by the manipulator itself is also an.

For intricate systems in which numerous components are
integrated,  such as  the YORI system,  it  is crucial  for  the
software stack to exhibit modularity, reusability, and possess
clear interfaces. These characteristics are essential  to ensure
the system’s expandability and to facilitate the seamless inte-
gration of diverse components.
As  illustrated in Fig. 6,  the YORI  system encompasses
five interfaces:  the User Interface (GUI), Camera Interface,
Controller Interface, Safety Interface, and Hardware Interface.
The necessity for these components to operate simultaneously
mandates  the  implementation of multithreading within the
software stack. This approach not only serves algorithmic

Fig. 7.  Flow diagram illustrating the control structure and messages used to c
communicates commands and statuses with the appliance manager through the
each local microcontroller one at a time over the shared usb bus by sending co
purposes but also promotes the equitable distribution of pro-
cessing tasks across the central processing unit (CPU). As a
result, shared memory and semaphores play a pivotal role in
coordinating data management among the various processes
running in parallel.
A. Manipulator Control
1) Control Method: Within  the YORI  system,  cooking
actions are primarily divided into two categories: transporting
motions, which entails the movement of ingredients and tools,
and grasping motions, which are dedicated to the latching
of tools and interactions with the cooking cell environment.
The transporting function predominantly utilizes the inverse
dynamics  feature of  the controller, whereas environmental
actions rely on the stiffness attribute of the impedance con-
troller. The deployment of an impedance controller enables
the manipulator arms to fluidly transition between states of
rigidity and flexibility. Such versatility ensures that the arms
remain rigid during the aerial transportation of ingredients, yet
become compliant when engaged in tool manipulation.
2) Payload Estimation: Given that proprioceptive actuators
can accurately compute joint torques via the current draw of
the actuators, the force at the end-effector can be determined
by subtracting the expected end-effector force, based on the
current configuration, from the actual end-effector force. This
can be used to accurately detect a weight being grasped by
the manipulator,  and in most  cases  this weight will be a
grasped tool. This provides valuable information for updating
the inertial characteristics of the arm for motion planning and
for fault detection. If the arm experiences a collision or fails
to properly grasp a tool, this can be detected via an increase
in force or a lack there of respectively.
3) Dual-Arm Control:  The dual-arm configuration was
divided into 2 kinematic chains starting from the torso or the
shoulder yaw joint depending on the task. Each arm has its
own controller, which allows each arm to be able to have
independent stiffness values based on the tasks.

9
ontrol the integrated appliances in the kitchen cell. The High Level Scheduler
systems shared memory. The appliance manager in turn communicates with
mmands tied to one of the unique microcontroller IDs.
For example, one arm can be compliant while tossing fries
for seasoning and the other arm can be stiff while transporting
an ingredient from one place to another. Being able to achieve
different stiffness was also helpful for tasks that require both
arms,  such as  the pan sweeping motion. The arm that  is
holding and moving the pan can be made stiff while the other
arm grasping a squeegee can be made compliant. This allows
for the exerting of a constant force downwards between the
rubber edge of the squeegee and the surface of the pan.
B. Tool Changer Control
To utilize the guiding pin on the tool changer and minimize
positional deviation,  it is beneficial to set a via point where
the end-effector can stop just behind the latching position. This
allows for the following docking motion to be in a straight line
to the tool plate. This via point is called the last point, and
the latching position at the tool plate is called the tool plate
point. The last point is set to be 5 cm away from the tool plate
point towards the interior of the kitchen cell. To grab a tool,
the tool changer opens its fingers at the last point and closes
them at the tool plate point.
In order to use the tool changer, an air directional control
valve was used to open and close the fingers of  the tool
changer. Each tool changer  is controlled via its own inde-
pendent control valve. The control valves are controlled via a
microcontroller, which was connected to the main computer
through USB. The microcontroller used was an Arduino UNO.
In order to make it respond faster to commands from the main
computer, the serial timeout value was set to 1 ms instead of
the default value of 1000 ms. The command is simply whether
to open or close the fingers.

The tool changer thread on the
main computer was running at 100 Hz.
C. Robotic Kitchen Control
The YORI kitchen system accommodates  its modularity
through a multi-tiered appliance management  system con-
sisting of  local  low level controllers, a mid-level appliance

manager, and a high level scheduling system. This is visually
laid out in Fig. 7.
At  the  lowest  level  are all of  the  individual  subsystem
microcontrollers. These directly control and monitor the in-
dividual appliances assigned to them. The microcontrollers
communicate over  a shared USB connection  to  the main
computer. Each microcontroller is assigned a unique ID, which
it reports to the computer when the system is initialized. The
system uses standardized commands such as Read, Command,
and Initialize to request  the current  status of  the attached
appliances, set a new goal for a given appliance, and set up
the serial connection respectively. Upon receiving a command,
the microcontroller will parse the received data and return a
success or error message to the main computer. Each appliance
has a unique set of input commands and status outputs, but
common to each appliance is the Busy status, which indicates
if  the appliance is in use or  if  it  is ready to accept a new
command. The Busy status  is primarily used by the  task
scheduler to determine if the current cooking task has been
completed and the system is ready to receive a new command.
The code controlling each appliance  is modular and non-
blocking. This allows multiple appliances to run in parallel
and for the controller to continue accessing the serial bus while
appliances are busy.
The appliance manager maintains the USB bus connections
and coordinates the transfer of data between the high level
scheduler and the microcontrollers. The system uses a shared-
memory database which can be read from and written to
by both the appliance manager and the high level scheduler.
The shared memory is divided into a command and a status
section for each appliance. In addition to the set of commands
and status variables unique to each appliance is an indicator
variable if  the scheduler has updated any of  the command
variables. At  regular  intervals  the appliance manager will
query each microcontroller for the current status of all of the
appliances it is associated with. This data will then be written
to its corresponding memory allotment. Then it will check if
the Update variable for each appliance has been changed from
0 to 1. If so it will take the newly given command data and
send it to the appropriate microcontroller. After the command
has been successfully sent the appliance manager will change
the shared memory variable back to 0 to indicate that  the
appliance command variables can be written to again by the
scheduler.
At the highest level is the scheduler which parses a recipe
file and communicates the required appliance commands to the
appliance manager through the shared memory buffer. Each
recipe the YORI system makes is defined by a series of action
commands to be sent  to a specific arm or appliance and a
related gate condition which determines when the next step
should be taken. This gate can be a timed delay, the completion
of an arm trajectory, or the changing of an appliance’s Busy
status. The scheduler reads the status variables and writes to
the command variables on the shared memory buffer in the
same way as the appliance manager. This interchange helps to
avoid errors caused by the two systems attempting to write to
the same variable simultaneously.

for taking out a fryer basket from the deep fryer. Blue line represents the
optimization objective with L∞ norm of jerk profile, green line represents
objective with L2 norm of jerk profile, and red line represents the objective
with combined L2 norms of jerk and weighted acceleration profile, α = 0.01.

D. Motion Planning
During the development of the manipulator’s motion plan-
ner, We first  implemented a bang-bang control approach as
was mentioned in the previous section. However, it was found
when a via point was added within the trajectory, the resulting
trajectory sometimes required very large accelerations leading
to undesirable positional overshooting. In order to avoid this,
the cost function was modified to a combination of jerk and
weighted acceleration norms. In order to match the dimensions
of both norms, we used the L2 norm and formulated the
following quadratic programming (QP) problem:
...
min  || x || + α||x¨||
x
(4)
s.t.  Ax = B
...
where x is the jerk profile and x¨ is the acceleration profile.
The constraint, Ax = B,  represents  the  initial  and final
conditions for the position, velocity, and acceleration profiles.
One such application for applying different optimization
objectives is shown in Fig. 8.

When the optimization objective
only consists of a minimum jerk profile with via points,  the
resulting trajectory saw significant overshoot, which could lead
to collision between the fryer basket and the shelf. Including
a  small weighted acceleration profile  to  the optimization
objective significantly improves the trajectory in terms of both
smoothness and the travel distance.
During the cooking operation, YORI uses  its camera at
the body to detect a fiducial marker on the targeted tool and
generates a goal position. YORI then runs this optimization-
based planner  to generate the reference trajectory, which is
then fed into either  the inverse dynamics controller or  the
impedance controller to execute the calculated trajectory.
E. Task Scheduling
In order  to find a feasible and efficient  solution to the
order and timing that  the required cooking actions should

take we took a job-shop scheduling approach and applied
Constraint Programming (CP) using Google OR-Tool’s CP-
SAT solver  [20]. The job-shop scheduling problem (JSSP)
has been explored since well before the twentieth century for
optimizing the output of complex systems. At  its core it  is
a combinatorial optimization problem which revolves around
distributing a set of tasks among machines such that the total
time to complete all  tasks, or  the makespan,  is minimized.
The ordering of  said tasks  is guided by a applied set of
constraints. The common ones defining the JSSP being: No
Overlap: each machine can handle no more than a single
task at a time, Precedence:  the tasks within a job must be
completed sequentially, and Continuity: once a task is started it
must be run to completion. To adapt this from a mathematical
scheduling solution to a real-world system a few additional
constraints will need to be added.
One such constraint being Concurrence or which machines
can be run simultaneously. Based on their location within the
kitchen cell, the manipulator can only activate or interact with
specific pairs of machines simultaneously. For example the
manipulator will not be able to simultaneously interact with
the deep fryer on the left shelf at the same time as the dicer
on the right shelf. Beyond the physical reach constraint, there
is also a limitation in that some machines need to be tended
to by the manipulator during its process. This can be seen in
the case of the food processor, when dicing potatoes one of
the manipulator arms needs to be holding a basket or other
container beneath the machine.
Other additional constraints relate to the realities of  the
cooking processes. An example of  this can be seen in the
Horizon constraint governing the acceptable completion times
of different  jobs and series of  tasks. Due to the customer
serving nature of our cooking system we will need to en-

sure that certain recipe orders are not unduly stretched out,
especially as additional orders are placed.

Another example
is creating a set horizon for finishing an order once certain
actions are undertaken. This is needed for not allowing a dish
to go cold after being removed from the oven before being
further processed or served.
To solve for the schedule with the shortest finishing time, we
transformed the above constraints into a series of linear, inter-
val, and logical constraints such that a constraint programming
approach could be applied. These are laid out in the Define
Constraints section of Algorithm 1. The CP-SAT solver was
chosen due to its flexibility and robustness to diverse logical
constraints. This solver employs a portfolio of parameters and
strategies which run in parallel to each other to each attempt
to locate a more optimal solution. It primarily utilizes a Lazy
Clause Generation (LCG) approach but also includes, and is
not  limited to, SAT search, LP Branching, and Pseudo-Cost
branching. Our scheduling problem primarily makes use of
boolean variables to assign the order of the task within the
schedule and is thus more suited for a CP solving approach
than a more traditional Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)
approach.
F. Dynamic Replanning
After a schedule has been generated the system will begin
to carry out the required tasks in their prescribed order. The
schedule is defined by a prescribed duration assigned to each
task, however in many cases this will be an approximation and
will have an acceptable completion window. As the system
operates it will signal  the start of  the next  tasks based on
the returned confirmation of completion of the previous task.
In the  ideal case  this would continue until all  tasks have
been successfully completed. However, with a  real world
implementation we need to be able to update to changing
conditions. These changes primarily come in the case of two
types of events. Either there has been some failure within the
system or an additional order has been placed. The response.

In this article, we have introduced the hardware and soft-
ware design processes of  an autonomous  robotic cooking

system utilizing cutting-edge technology. This system’s de-
velopment went beyond mere simulations and lab environ-
ments. The system was fully operational and showcased at a
convention in Korea. To enhance its reliability, we simplified
the system, which allowed us to successfully demonstrate the
continuous cooking of steak frites numerous times throughout
the convention. The system’s modularity and strategic simpli-
fications enabled this achievement, despite having only one
day for preparation at  the venue, underscoring the system’s
exceptional reliability and efficiency.
While we have demonstrated the effectiveness of simplify-
ing the system, modular design of robotic kitchens, and the
potential of the dual-arm manipulator equipped with proprio-
ceptive actuators, achieving a fully autonomous cooking sys-
tem remains an ambitious goal. Future research directions that
could contribute towards fully realizing an ideal autonomous
cooking system include the following categories.
1) Robotic Cell Layout:  In the realm of optimization for
robotic kitchens, constraints related to a robot’s kinematics
often dominate the design considerations. Designing within
compact and complex environments, such as kitchens, presents
inherent  challenges when only kinematics  are  taken  into
account. However,  the  task becomes even more daunting
due to the underutilization of numerous heuristic terms. For
instance, when considering the placement of appliances the
system ought  to factor  in that some appliances may splash
grease or oil  such and should be positioned lower,  steam-
releasing units higher,  and ensuring that  appliances which
generate significant heat  should not  located beneath other
electronic devices. These constraints illustrate the complexity
of  real kitchen design. Additionally,  the energy efficiency
of manipulator movements ought  to be considered,  further
complicating the design process.
Adopting a modular approach, where kitchen components
are designed in segments and optimized during assembly, can
offer a more efficient solution. This methodology, reminiscent
of assembling Legos or modular  furniture,  allows  for  the
incorporation of manufacturing constraints and the previously
mentioned constraints into the design phase. By focusing on
the robotic manipulator’s kinematics and energy efficiency
during the assembly of these modular kitchen units, it becomes
feasible to construct a customized kitchen space that aligns
with user preferences with relative ease. This process not only
streamlines the design but also ensures that the final layout is
both functional and efficient, demonstrating the potential of
modular designs in overcoming the challenges of robotic cell
layout in kitchen environments.
2) Task & Motion Planning: To maximize the utility of
modular kitchens and dual-arm manipulators, it is imperative
to develop methodologies for  transferring new recipes  into
tasks and trajectories  that are compatible with the current
kitchen layout. Moreover, the execution of these planned tasks
necessitates research into ensuring that  the dual arms avoid
self-collision and effectively navigate  the  intricate kitchen
environment through appropriate motion planning. This aspect
of research is crucial, as it not only facilitates the seamless
integration of innovative recipes into the autonomous cooking
process but also enhances the system’s operational efficiency

13
and safety within complex kitchen settings.
3) Food Quality Control: A significant advantage of au-
tonomous cooking systems is their ability to provide food of
consistent quality anytime, anywhere. To fully leverage this
advantage, the development of food quality control technolo-
gies is equally essential. Such technologies need to be capable
of verifying the freshness of ingredients and determining the
completion status of cooking tasks. However,  the vast array
of ingredients and the multitude of factors that influence their
condition make it challenging to assess freshness and cooking
status through mere visual inspection or to control cooking by
time alone.
The application of machine  learning technologies holds
promise for overcoming these challenges. By training models
with data from various sensors, in particular those measuring
the gases emitted from the ingredients and cooking processes,
these systems can address the complexities associated with in-
gredient freshness and the nuances of cooking. This approach
not only promises to enhance the precision of food quality con-
trol but also supports the adaptive capabilities of autonomous
cooking systems, enabling them to accommodate a wide range
of  ingredients and recipes.

Furthermore,  the integration of
machine  learning could facilitate  real-time adjustments  to
cooking parameters based on the specific characteristics of
each ingredient, thereby ensuring optimal food quality and re-
ducing waste. This dynamic method of quality control, rooted
in advanced technology, marks a significant step forward in
the evolution of autonomous culinary systems, aiming to meet
the high standards of  consistency and excellence  in food
preparation.
4)  Ingredient Preparation & Delivery: The integration of
a well-developed system for supplying ingredients, water, and
sauces is paramount to enhancing the utility of robotic cooking
systems. If such a system could be designed similarly to meal
prep delivery services, where ingredients are pre-portioned
and ready for cooking,  the efficiency and applicability of
robotic cooking systems could be significantly increased. This
approach not only streamlines the cooking process but also en-
sures consistency and quality in meal preparation. This aligns
with the broader goal of consistently delivering uniformly
high-quality food anywhere and anytime. Consequently, ad-
vancing the infrastructure for food preparation and delivery
directly complements the technological advancements in au-
tonomous cooking systems, making the seamless operation of
such systems in diverse environments a more attainable goal.
